content.full_text:
RAvI MEIITA
Cii Al II MA N
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
January 9,
1996
Peter A.
Bagatelos
Bagatelos
& Fadem
601 California St., Ste.
1801
San Francisco,
CA
94108
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1-95-364
Dear Mr.
Bagatelos:
You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the Actt).h/
You have not
identified the client on whose behalf you are requesting this
advice.
Therefore,
we will treat your letter as a request for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
!
QUESTION
You have asked whether a slate mailer organization
(“SMO”)
is
receiving contributions when the owners and promoters pay start—up
expenses for the SNO,
or later provide the operating funds to
allow the
SMO to function toward its goal of selling advertising
space for a profit.
CONCLUSION
Payments for start—up or operating expenses of a business
entity that qualifies as a
SMO are not “contributions” under the
Act.
Therefore, the
SMO will not also become a recipient
committee merely by virtue of receiving these payments.
11
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code- unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code cf Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
-12X i Sri-ui
St
ii 50(1, SM .-ii w. (‘i
riiit’u ‘ ‘J5514
(01(i) 3—50(i()


Peter A. Bagatelos
Our File No.
1—95—364
Page
2
FACTS
Your clients own and operate a slate mailer organization.
The
SMO is a commercial profit making entity.
The
SMO sells
advertising space on slate mailers to candidates and committees
that wish to purchase such space.
Certain expenses of producing
the slate mailers are incurred well before campaigns are solicited
to participate.
These include salaries and payments for design
and layout,
as well as expenses incurred in soliciting candidates
and committees to participate in the mailer.
In such instances, the owners and promoters of the
SMO put up
their own funds to pay the costs associated with preparing,
producing, promoting and ultimately distributing the slate mailer.
ANALYSIS
Individuals and entities that qualify as “committees” under
the Act are required to file periodic reports disclosing
contributions received and expenditures made.
(Section 84100,
g.)
The term “conunittee” is defined in Section 82013 as
follows:
“Committee” means any person or combination of
persons who directly or indirectly does any of the
following:
(a)
Receives contributions totaling one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more in a calendar
year.
(b)
Makes independent expenditures totaling
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more in a calendar
year; or
(c)
Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars
($10;0b0)
or more in a calendar year to or
at the behest of candidates or committees....
A “contribution” is a payment made for political purposes for
which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor.
(Section 82015.)
A payment is made for political purposes if it
is for the purpose of influencing the action of the voters for or
against the nomination or election of a candidate or the
qualification or passage of a measure.
(Regulation 18215.)
Separate disclosure requirements are imposed on persons that
receive payments totaling $500 or more in a calendar year for the
purpose of sending slate mailers supporting or opposing four or
more candidates or ballot measures.
(Sections 82048.3,
82048.4.)
Section 82048.4 defines “slate mailer organization” as follows:
(a)
“Slate mailer organization” means,
except
as provided in subdivision (b),
any person who,
directly or indirectly,
does all of the following:
(1)
Is involved in the production of one or
more slate mailers and exercises control over the


Peter A. Bagatelos
Our File No.
1—95—364
Page
3
selection of the candidates and measures to be
supported or opposed in the slate mailers.
(2)
Receives or is promised payments totaling
five hundred dollars
($500) or more in a calendar
year for the production of one or more slate
mailers.
***
(c)
The production and distribution of slate
mailers by a slate mailer organization shall not be
considered making contributions or expenditures for
purposes of subdivision
(b)
or
(c)
of Section
82013.
If a slate mailer organization makes
contributions or expenditures other than by
producing or distributing slate mailers,
and it
reports those contributions and expenditures
pursuant to Sections 84218 and 84219,
no additional
campaign reports shall be required of the slate
mailer organization pursuant to Section 84200 or
84200.5.
Generally, payments received by a slate mailer organization
are not “contributions” because it is presumed the candidates and
committees that pay to appear on the slate mailer are receiving
“full and adequate consideration.”
(Finley Advice Letter,
No.
A-
92-219.)
Likewise,
payments made by the owners of a business
entity for start-up and operating expenses, for wh±ch they intend
to receive full and adequate consideration in the form of profits
from the production of slate mailers, would not be deemed
“contributions” under the Act.
3
?
If you have any questions concerning this letter, please
contact me at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
&a &4(
By:
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
The Finley Advice Letter goes on to state that payments
received by a slate mailer organization to help defray the general
costs of publishing a slate mailer are contributions because no
consideration is provided in exchange for the payment.
However,
in Finley, the donors were making payments for political purposes
to assist in publishing a pro—choice voter guide and there was no
understanding that they would recoup their expenses in the form of
profits from the
SMO.
